content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Best movies where once it starts, it never lets go?

**Score:** 91
**Body:** I'm talking movies like Dunkirk where the oppressive score never allows you to take a breath, Evil Dead 2013 where the sound design is so intense you can't take your eyes off the screen, Fury Road's action sequences that captivate you from the very first second until the credits roll.

Any other recommendations that fit this category? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I only watched it once, but Uncut Gems felt like a 2 hour long panic attack.

Edit: might as well add, I saw the movie in theaters at the first showing on New Years Day. I told myself that pre-buying a ticket to a late morning movie would ensure I didn’t rot in bed all day. I don’t know what the best circumstance for seeing this movie is, but the worst might be hungover after a night of partying in a theater with surround sound. 

I still stuck through it and it’s a great film.
  - I quit watching after about 10 minutes. It was just too manic for me.
2. District 9, recently rewatched it, it's a non-stop high-adrenaline action packed chaotic, very well made,  mayhem.
3. apocalypto
4. *The Raid: Redemption* is almost entirely non-stop action. *1917* is also really good for this because it's filmed as basically one continuous shot with no time jumps or cuts. It really makes you uneasy and kind of exhausted by the end because it feels like you never get to pause for a break. 
5. 1917 feels like one continuous take for 90%+ of the movie. I think it's a very engrossing film and keeps you on the edge of your seat throughout it.
6. Can’t believe nobody has said Train To Busan yet - what an experience
7. Crank

Crank: High Voltage

Die Hard

Hardcore Henry 

Upgrade
8. mad max fury road, the most left to right to left movie ever...

in movie language u go left to right at the start then as ure hero returns home u go the oppsite, that movie puts its foot down from frame 1 to end frame its like whipneck speed all the way left to right...then litterlt they turn and go back to the start via a epic action
9. Whiplash is the movie you are looking for here.

## 2. Mid-tier movies from the 90s

**Score:** 55
**Body:** For an unknown reason I can't watch tv series anymore, I'm in a reading slump, I CANNOT spend another night doom scrolling reddit. (Although I love you guys)

I need mid tier 90s movies because I watch 'good' movies with my partner (with whom I don't live). We're going through the 70s and 80s right now hence the 90s. 

Any genre except kid's stuff (family friendly ok) and noir. I do have a soft spot for scifi, horror, thrillers and super tedious procedurals. 

Also I hate Dustin Hoffman with all my heart. 

UPDATE : you are all so so kind, my list is getting ridiculously long (and I love that) I'm so sorry I'm not being able to thank everyone personnaly but I AM noting down everything and I WILL watch em all.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Encino man (top tier for me, but I recognize it’s objective flaws)
  - If you watch Encino Man, you have to do a double feature with Biodome.
    - I prefer Son in Law.
2. All the farrely brothers stuff like Dumb and Dumber, Myself and Irene and Kingpin are hysterical.


Face Off, The Rock, Cliffhanger and Point Break are really fun action movies.
  - You rarely hear anyone mention Kingpin but it had me laughing so hard I was crying when I watched it in the theater.
3. Sneakers (1992)
4. Blast from the Past (nice little rom com with Brendan Fraser and Alicia Silverstone)

The Fugitive (thriller/action with Harrison Ford) (note: this is probably not mid tier. In fact, it has 96% on rotten. But, it’s awesome)

Dumb and dumber (this is the funniest movie of all time but certainly not “critically acclaimed”)
5. Tango and Cash
6. Couple 90's Favorites: 

Anaconda

Deep Blue Sea

Demolition Man

Speed

No one will EVER accuse them of being "great movies" but they're profoundly entertaining.
  - Demolition man holds up pretty well.

## 3. Movies like " Let the right one in (2008)"

**Score:** 21
**Body:** It has been a while since I’ve seen a wonderful horror/romance film like 'Let the Right One In' (2008). Do you have any good suggestions for a nice horror/romance movie? Any language is fine.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bones and All
2. Only Lovers Left Alive is about vampire lovers - great mood and vibe, not a lot of horror.

What We Do In the Shadows has an incredible erotic dance tho, not to be missed!
3. *Bram Stoker’s Dracula* (1992)

*Mary Shelley’s Frankenstein* (1994)

*Dracula* (1979)

And if you want a television series, *Penny Dreadful* (2014) has some gorgeous and sumptuous romance elements, as well.
4. A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night. It’s eerie, slow, kinda poetic....same lonely, haunting vibe with a strange sense of tenderness underneath.
  - yes really good!! +1
5. Only lovers left alive (2013)

Crimson peak (2015)

A girl walks alone at night (2014)

Thirst (2009)

The Untamed (La region salvaje) (2016)

  
What we do in the shadows - movie first then the show.
6. *A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night*... just a tad more art-y than *Let the Right One In*, but great nonetheless!
7. Humanist Vampire Seeking Consenting Suicidal Person 
8. Warm Bodies although not as good as Let the Right One In
9. Sinners

## 4. Any movies out there about someone who makes a bad mistake and hurts the people he cares about, then tries to keep living and being a better person?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I’m just in a bad spot right now where I’ve hurt the people I care about in a bad way. I know I’m not an irredeemable, terrible monster and that my mistakes don’t define me, but it’s hard. 

Any movies about someone in a similar spot? Someone who’s hurt the people he cares about in an irreversible way. He’s burnt bridges that can’t be rebuilt. And all he can do is keep living. All he can do is try to be better in the future and not repeat those mistakes.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 'The Fisher King,' from 1991. Its unique storyline rooted in Arthurian legend, is steeped in trauma and redemption with great emotional depth. Great film for one who can relate to a man carrying a heavy load for something he can't unchange and by helping a lost soul, finds salvation himself. Directed by Terry Gilliam, it's unconventional to say the least, and filled with humor. Stars Jeff Bridges, Robin Williams and Mercedes Ruehl, who won an Academy Award for her performance.

I sincerely hope that you find your way to forgving yourself.
  - Fisher King is an excellent suggestion 

OP, humans mess up. It’s what we do. But the true test is about actively learning to be better especially to people who care about you. Some people will forgive you. Some won’t (and they do have the right not to accept an apology because they are processing their own grief over the situation) 

the subreddit r/decidingtobebetter 
Might help give you support and tools to help you. The fact that you recognize and are facing what you did is a huge step in changing your path for the better.
2. The Royal Tennenbaums
3. i haven't watched it myself yet but A Good Person starring Florence Pough looks like exactly that. 

the tv show Fleabag was funny but also deeply emotional. one of the best written shows i've ever seen. 

Everything Must Go. it's funny. but not a comedy. it's a great example that Will Farell should do way more dramatic work. 

Basketball Diaries. based on a true story about addiction and recovery. one of DiCaprio's earliest roles that got him significant attention



Steve Carell's character in Little Miss Sunshine?
4. Les Miserables
5. Manchester by the sea. Hell of a mistake 
6. Atonement?
7. Roman J Israel, Esq
8. Worst Person in the World? I recommend this movie every chance I get.
9. The Pawnbroker

## 5. obscure horror/thriller with amazing visuals/cinematography

**Score:** 16
**Body:** movies like this ive watched:

\- mullholland drive 

\- under the silver lake

\- the substance

\- midsommar

it'll be amazing if they leave me traumatised and confused <3

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dellamorte Dellamore (aka Cemetery Man). The last gasp of classic Italian horror, and it's pure eye candy.
2. 'Valerie and Her Week of Wonders' (1970)

'Daughters of Darkness' (1971)

'The Hourglass Sanatorium' (1973)
3. The new Suspiria was awesome.
4. Antichrist. It's hauntingly beautiful...and fucked.
5. Lost Highway
6. Ravenous (1999)

Possession (1980)
7. The Cell 
It didn’t get very good reviews at the time, but was a beautiful and I thought really well done and frightening movie.
8. Dude or dudette, "Under the Silver Lake" is hot stuff!! 

That film is ALL THE WAY under people's radar. But not yours (apparently) and certainly not mine. So, right on for digging that.

Here are a few more, in both genres individually; including a couple of crossovers:

1. Under the Skin (2013)

2.  Lost Highway (1997)

3. The Innocents (2021)
4. Stay (2006)

5. Thief (1981)

6. The Keep (1983)

7. Blood Simple (1984)

8. The Ninth Gate (1999)

9. Eye of the Beholder (1999)

10. Melancholia (2011)
9. Cronos (Guillermo del Toro’s first feature movie; in Spanish)

Nightmare Alley (del Toro remake); one of the most visually striking movies I’ve ever seen
10. Irréversible (2002) - "*Events over the course of one traumatic night in Paris unfold in reverse-chronological order.*"

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What is a really great movie that don't get the credit it deserve or is underappreciated?

**Score:** 1742
**Body:** Going back a few years, I will say Mel Gibson’s Apocalypto.

I think it got a lot of bad press because of some personal embarrassments Mel got into at the time, but the movie is just amazing, and worth reconsideration if you haven’t seen it. It is fresh, brutal, immersive, it takes you away to another time and place extremely foreign to modern Western viewers, yet totally relatable. It had an amazing feeling of authenticity, not so much like a Hollywood movie, but more like channeling a Mayan jungle warrior from 500 years ago and peering over his shoulder. Look closely at the intensity of this single frame of the movie. Now expand that in your mind to a full length feature film.



### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Apocalypto was one of the funnest movie theater experiences of my adult life. I was so engrossed in the action and beautiful scenery that I was blown away by the ending.
  - That ending -- wow!!!
2. Lord of War
  - When he sits there and describes to Ethan Hawke what’s about to go down and everything that ensues, one of my favorite scenes to watch 🤌
3. Kung Fu Hustle
4. The temple scene is amazing. Just watched it in 4k and it was awesome, beautiful film. 
5. Margin call
  - I've worked in finance and banking for decades and everything in this movie rings true. Watching this, it's clear the filmmakers actually knew people on Wall Street. This is the work people actually do on Wall Street. Every character is someone I've met in my career and (with a few exceptions) the way they speak is the way people I work with speak every day. "The Wolf of Wall Street" is not actually about Wall Street and "The Big Short" is a bit too on-the-nose for me. If you want to watch a movie about the financial crisis, "Margin Call" is the ticket.
6. Collateral

Tom Cruise is amazing as Vincent, an antagonist you can't help but like and even root for. The plot and action are great, and like Michael Mann's other badass film Heat, it uses the actual sounds of the firearms.

Most importantly, it contains the greatest shooting scene of all time. Sorry John Wick fans, it's just true. Watch the film, trust me.
7. I like Apocalypto, too.

The Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Robert Ford comes to mind immediately. A beautiful film with great performances and a fantastic score. Some people ( like myself) absolutely love this film, but it’s probably too arthouse for a mainstream audience.

## 2. Rewatched The Thing (1982) last night and oh man..

**Score:** 1209
**Body:** Cosmic horror has never been cooler than this. A young Kurt Russel wielding a flame thrower,
shotgun, and sticks of dynamite against a deadly alien mimicking force. As a lover of imaginative practical effects, I’m absolutely satisfied rewatching this every October from now on.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's so goddamned good
  - “I don’t know what it is, but it’s all weird and pissed off”
    - *Damnit Child's, torch it!*
2. "I know you gentlemen have been through a lot, but when you find the time, I'd rather not spend the rest of this winter TIED TO THIS FUCKING COUCH!"

Literally one of the best lines and delivery of said line I have ever heard anyone do, live or film.
3. It’s my favorite movie of all time
  - Everyone in it is so intelligent too. A rare horror movie where people are largely doing rational, sensible stuff in the face of inexplicable eldritch terror. 
    - These are the best kind of horror movies. Event Horizon and Aliens fit that description too in my opinion.
      - I love that whole “gotta get the hell out of this messed up shit” style. Event Horizon is beautiful with that. 

“I have no intention of leaving her, Doctor. I will take the Lewis and Clark to a safe distance, and then I will launch TAC missiles at the Event Horizon until I'm satisfied she's vaporized. Fuck this ship!”

Another good contender there is 1408, with Sam Jackson who knows entirely that something is fucked up beyond sanity in that room, and spends like 10 minutes actually begging John Cusack not to stay there (“it’s an *evil* fucking room”). 

And Cusack himself, initially arrogant cause he visits these “haunted” places for a living and they’re never actually haunted, does his best to try to leave the nightmare once he realizes it’s real. I love that movie so much. 
4. Epic movie. One of my favourite.

It's on at cinema next week (re-run) and I'm off to see it!
5. One of those films that never weakens no matter how many times you see it. It’s just so damn clean and effective.

## 3. Chernobyl is by far the creepiest thing I've ever seen

**Score:** 955

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The quote from Legasov where he interrupts the committee meeting to try and instill the weight of the disaster is what always comes to mind first: “…That's three million billion trillion bullets in the... in the air we breathe, the water we drink, the food we eat. Most of these bullets will not stop firing for 100 years. Some of them, not for 50,000 years.”

[IMDB Chernobyl Quotes](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt8482972/quotes/?item=qt6746048)

More quotes if anyone is feeling like re-experiencing.
  - Most of the quotes from this show are either deeply dark and funny or straight chilling.
    - The scene in the apartment where Legasov blurts out "... and we'll be dead in five years!" is so good.

And Stellan Skarsgard conveying the absolute doom he's just been faced with... absolute cinema.
  - For me the turning poin was when the scientist in charge of the crisis casually says to the bureaucrat that they won't survive 5 years after being exposed to the radiation, and only then the other guy realises how serious the whole thing is.
  - "We are dealing with something that has never happened on this planet." Absolutely terrifying. The contrary between how small we are compared to the planet, but how destructive we are to it.
2. Chilling maybe? Creepy doesnt say it for me tbh
  - The clicking of the Geiger counters on the dudes who went underground was particularly creepy & chilling. Just a constant clicking that rattled my bones. 😬
3. Im watching it again right now. I'm glad they decided to explain the truth, esp considering it could've easily died with them. The fact that the Kremlin decided to make this man into a ghost during his final days is horrible though.
4. That’s like saying Apollo 13 was the scariest movie I’ve ever seen
  - Apollo 13 is one of the most nerve-wracking movies I've ever seen, personally.

## 4. What movie villain made you feel uneasy and scared with their ruthlessness and the intensity of their performance? I'll go first:

**Score:** 511
**Body:** J.K. Simmons as Terence Fletcher in Whiplash (2014).

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Joaquin Phoenix as Commodus in Gladiator. He NAILED that character!
  - I was 14 when that came out. He scared the shit out of me. A true emotionally fueled monster.
2. Robert DeNiro in Cape Fear
3. Javier in No Country for Old Men.
4. Jake Gyllenhaal in Nightcrawler. I’m now convinced that Jake truly is a psychopath in real life. No one is that good of an actor.
5. Whiplash is such a good movie.
6. Christopher Walz In Inglorious Basterds. 
7. Anthony Hopkins in Silence of the Lambs was so good, but so unsettling.
8. This guy in oz
9. Ralph Fiennes in "Schindler's List."

## 5. Oct 16th selection. This long forgotten gem.

**Score:** 471

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It is a classic. Love it. Gonna watch it soon. Just finished the original trilogy evil dead too all classics :)
  - I watched Army of Darkness immediately before this. I got hit in the head from a falling black walnut this morning while feeding my chickens and spent most of the day inside on the couch with a big ass bump on my head and a splitting headache.
2. I remember seeing this in the theater when it came out. It’s amazing.
  - Not going to lie. I am surprised it was in theaters. I thought it was a straight to DVD movie.
    - I actually saw it in a theater in Royal Oak Michigan and Bruce Campbell was answering questions after
 Don't remember if this was the world premiere or not.
3. The penultimate documentary about the struggle of Elvis Presley and JFK combining forces to combat the entrenched and corrupt Egyptian political regime.
4. “… I’m thinkin’ with SAND!!”
5. Fuckin love this movie
6. Such a fun movie! Anyone with a b-grade movie sense of humor should delight in this
7. I remember renting it when video stores were still a thing. My wife and child left town for a week and upon first viewing I knew it was a classic.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. On my 3rd viewing of One Battle After Another, I noticed how well the entire opening 30-ish minutes works without sound

**Score:** 44
**Body:** I make narrative and documentary films and am at the theater constantly. For my first short film, I gave myself the restriction of no dialogue- nobody could talk. It wasn't great but it still worked, and it was a "success" because I learned a lot about visual storytelling and communicating through images and editing, basic film 101 stuff. I'm very aware of this in film, and like a lot of people, find that the more a film can communicate visually, the stronger the filmmaking, to oversimplify my stance. 

I noticed with OBAA, that the entire "prologue", or opening half hour (not sure exactly how long it is) works almost entirely without sound. People have mentioned that it's essentially a very long montage, which is incredibly impressive in it's own right, but I find it even more impressive that PTA has become so smooth-handed with the craft that he can set up the characters, the relationships, the conflicts, all of this through image and editing. Even through body language- the turning point scene, in which the French 75 rob the bank. Junglepussy gives her monologue and then you hear the gunshot, see Perfidia shooting the guard. Even if you couldn't hear the gunshot, the scene still works, Junglepussy's body language is enough to visually show the tonal shift. 

I won't go on with every detail, but the dynamics between the characters are so well established through body language, how they are framed in the shot, the editing. 

This seems like introductory film stuff, yet it's so expertly executed here and it also stands out to me how rare it is nowadays to see a film, especially of this scale and budget, have such a handle on core filmmaking techniques. Most movies are ESTABLISHING SHOT/WIDE, medium over, reverse medium over, closeeup, closeup. Rinse and repeat. There's so few establishing/wides in this film. It's really impressive.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think someone could write a book on just the openings of PTA movies. If he’s a master at anything, it’s how he pulls you in at the start. 

There Will Be Blood and The Master are very similar in the ways you’re describing and it was nice to see PTA go back to those impulses for the opening of One Battle!

Comment length comment length comment length comment length
  - The opening to Magnolia is so insanely good. The “These strange things happen all the time.” line leading into the Aimee Mann song is perfect.
    - The "One Is The Lonliest Number" scene in Magnolia is maybe one of my favorite scenes of all time. There's just so much happening all at once, but it never feels overwhelming or illegible.
  - What an incredibly insightful comment. Haven’t ever thought about why I like his movies so much but you’re absolutely right—he sets the hook perfectly right from the jump
    - I’ll take it a step further and point out the use of score in setting up the emotions and tone of his films is remarkable.

The somber circus melody that plays over the opening of Boogie Nights, before we’re blasted with ‘Best of my Love,’ suggests the sadness beneath the fun of the story’s surface, and it’s before we’ve even started the journey.

There’s a similar kind of priming that happens in the opening of Phantom Thread. The eery violins that start the film suggest the oddness that the story is headed for, but then bleed effortlessly into that beautiful main theme. It’s so casual that you forget about it entirely, until the story takes its turns and the same eery score returns, and you remember this is where we were set up to go right from the beginning. 

It’s truly second-to-none. Brilliant cinema.
2. I consider myself a PTA fan and had seen all his films including OBAA recently which was great, but I had skipped his 3 movies after The Master for whatever reason. What’s most worth seeing next out of the three:

Inherent Vice

Phantom Thread

Licorice Pizza
  - I think they're all solid movies but Phantom Thread is a pure masterpiece to me. One of my absolute favorites, of his and in general. 

Licorice Pizza and IV are both solid movies and I love things about them and enjoy them both immensely but they're hangout movies, vibe movies. I'd say I'm more likely to put on Phantom Thread at any point than the other two, and have seen it maybe a dozen times.
  - Licorice Pizza is fun, Phantom Thread is a slow burn, and Inherent Vice is confusing but entertaining.

## 2. Serious question: Is the Tron franchise cursed or something?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** I swear that in 2010 most people didn't like Tron: Legacy, and you could hear from everyone how the plot and acting are really bad in the movie, and that the only good thing about the movie is the visuals and music.

  
It's 2025 now, and besides the fact that the people started overpraising Tron: Legacy. I mean, the acting and the plot are actually good in the movie, but nowhere near the Oscars level, right? It's just decent overall.

  
It's Tron: Ares now, and... I didn't watch the movie yet, but guess what? I hear all the same things for some reason. The visuals and music are good, but the plot and acting are really bad. Although some point out that it's not that bad, but like both the plot and acting are very mid-ish. And I just can't grasp why it is like that with these movies?



Is that Tron's franchise curse or something? Was it like that with the first Tron in 1982? Will people be saying in 10-15 years something like "damn Tron Ares is so good and underappreciated, the visuals and the music are top notch, Jared Leto is not so bad"?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The original Tron wasn't a huge hit, but it was a cult classic due to the totally unique visuals and the astoundingly good Wendy Carlos soundtrack. Legacy wasn't a huge hit, but made some money based on the updated visuals and great Daft Punk soundtrack. I think there's just not a ton of nostagia to tap into, 1982 was a long, long time ago.
  - It’s this mostly. The Tron series of movies have never been amazing stories but they’re visually interesting showpieces that capture your imagination. A lot of people forget or don’t know that when the first movie came out that was a very time and place thing when the burgeoning personal computer market was starting to take hold and the idea of them in and of themselves was captivating to most people who barely knew what a computer was and likely had no personal experience with one. So just the concept of what Tron is as a concept is/was interesting. Plus the visuals were something that was still new and hadn’t been done that widely on that sort of scale. The plot of Tron itself is nothing amazing and it’s kind of slow and boring overall. 

Tron Legacy picked it up with a more moving and interesting plot, that was sort of trying to grasp at saying *something* but still, it was another movie that was really visually interesting but not doing a lot plot wise. Plus Legacy banks on you having a lot of reverence for the first movie to begin with. And if you don’t have that then it’s kind of whatever to you anyways. I haven’t seen Ares yet but I completely understand the reaction and lack of interest based on the previous two movies. Tron is a series of movies I personally love, but understand why they aren’t these massively successful and beloved films.
    - Legacy has a few moments where it almost... almost... turns into something incredible. Michael Sheen yelling "Bow to our father!" and The Dude doing his little reality warp in the club show how it could easily turn into a Matrix-style philosophical action movie... but ultimately it's a good looking kids film, that isn't bad, but it's also not really very high stakes, not enough is explored and so it's a bit bland in the end. It's not a bad movie, it's just... a movie that exists.
  - 1982 was a crazy year for Sci-Fi

E.T.

The Wrath Of Khan

And these next two sadly flopped, but

The Thing

And

Blade Runner
2. It's a mediocre movie with spectacular visuals and a spectacular soundtrack that not a lot of people are interested in. Leto gets a lot of hate, but him, Lee, and Peters are quite good in this, the script is just paper thin. So not cursed, it's just nobody really cares.
  - Tron is the Treasure Planet of live action sci-fi. Reddit millennial insist its this hidden gem that needs a second chance, but it failed for a reason. 

The somewhat unique ideas and aesthetic are *all* a lot of these old "hidden gems" have going for them. They failed because as stories, they're just not that good. And a fun aesthetic isn't enough to counter that.
    - If terminator had a weak story, it would be TRON. 

TRON is Waterworld. People remember the gripping visuals. But that’s it. 

Franchises like Alien, Terminator, Mad Max, etc. all had very gripping visuals but also a vast and interesting world with memorable characters and interesting stories. That’s why they endure. And that’s why with Waterworld, people only remember that it was a world with water. That’s why with TRON, people just remember the motorcycle chase scene.
3. It’s funny, because it seems that the 80’s Tron was never more than a niche sci-fi nerd favorite, but soon enough Disney figured “you know what, there are people out there who liked Tron. Let’s try to bring in the young adult male audience with a new one.”

Then, as you said, Tron Legacy came out and was embraced minimally, mainly starting once Letterboxd started getting really popular. So again, Disney said, “you know what, there are people out there who liked Tron Legacy. Let’s try to bring in the young adult male audience with a new one.”

All three movies seem to operate under the assumption that everyone loves Tron and will gobble anything in that universe up. When in reality, it’s always been a niche thing.
  - Yeah. It's less that the franchise is "cursed" and more that it's a strange little miracle that this niche cult classic got turned into a Disney franchise at all and they keep acting like it's something it's not. I have a soft spot for Tron and so I don't really mind because hey, they keep making em, but if a Tron movie were ever a big hit I'd be shocked.
4. I think what it boils down to is simply that Tron, as a franchise, is just really fucking stupid. I mean sure, in '82 the vfx was pretty impressive and the concept of digital 'spaces' was novel, but beyond that there's not much else left to work with. Which is probably intentional because they just wanted to make a colorful cyber cyber lightbike movie.


So writers are left trying to squeeze water out of a stone for the 'sequels' and it shows. 


Additionally, Leto has no charisma. You could argue that Bridges helped pull in an audience for the last one, but Leto? Eh...

---
